Note: There are no courses, distances or bearings for the first 10 hours of this trip, no bearings or positions for the rest of this trip, and the last three hours have no courses or distances. Take these positions with a grain of salt. I looked at several other ships to get an idea of how fast they came down the Delaware River: anthing between 6 and 11 knots, with 9 being common but four trips being made between 6 and 7.5 kts. So, I went with 8.
The pressures for the PM period are very suspicious. I didn't know how to fix them.

People: 1515: At 3:15 P. M. 100 Pdr Parrott gun burst at the 37th fire killing the following men, viz: Ludwick E. Witt (Sea), John Hill (Sea), James Cormick (Sea), James T. Duffy (Lds), James T. Ward (Lds), Chas. G. Stiles (Ord sea), James W. M. Miller (Lds) and wounding Edward R. Borman (Qr Mr), Chas Brown 3rd (Sea), William Jones (Ord sea), James Williams (Ord Sea), Wm. H. Milliken (Ord Sea), L. G. Vassallo (Act Vol Lieut), Shultz B. Martin (Lds), Jacob Holt (Lds), Cornelius Collins (Private Marines), William Sinton (2nd class boy) and Phineas B. Snyder (Ord Sea).
0200: William Sinton (2nd class Boy) died from the effects of wounds caused by the bursting of the 100 Pdr.

Note: 1100: Got Underway. Took our place in the line of battle, standing towards the enemy's batteries.
